content:
TITLE: Meta AI vs CDC: I Fed It the Outbreak Data. It Broke.
SUBTITLE: 17 states. 145 cases. AI failed at #146.

2 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

IMAGE_CAPTION = "Meta AI vs CDC Data: Testing AI against 17 states of outbreak data."145 cases. 17 states. 20 hospitalizations. And one parasite causing “explosive, watery diarrhea” across the U.S.
The CDC confirmed it in June 2026. No international travel. No deaths. But a clear signal: something in the U.S. food chain is contaminated. The FDA investigation is still open. The source? Still a mystery.
So I did what most newsrooms won’t. I pulled the raw CDC tables and fed them straight into Meta’s Llama 4.
The goal wasn’t to rewrite the CDC alert. It was to see if AI could actually _think_ like an outbreak analyst. Can it spot the pattern? Can it flag the next state? Can it name the food?
I spent 3 years covering CDC outbreaks. I’ve tested AI on 4 past outbreaks. This one was different. Llama 4 did something in 30 seconds that took the CDC weeks to publish. It also hit a wall that every AI hype video ignores.
The result splits into 2 parts: what AI got right, and where it failed hard.
If you think AI can “predict” outbreaks from thin air, you’ll want to read this. If you cover health, tech, or data, you need to see the exact prompts I used and the 3 real workflows that saved time.
The CDC still leads. But AI might be closer than you think.
I broke down the full test, the data, and what I tell my own family to do right now here: https://worldcutruygdski.blogspot.com/2026/07/meta-ai-cyclospora-cdc-outbreak-17-states.html
